original_registry_entry.issuing_organisation: United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
original_registry_entry.version: CDC healthcare-provider clinical overview; page dated July 15, 2024
original_registry_entry.population: People with suspected ringworm or dermatophyte infection, including skin, scalp, foot, groin, and nail presentations.
original_registry_entry.clinical_setting: Clinical recognition, exposure assessment, and diagnostic testing for suspected fungal skin or nail infection.
original_registry_entry.applicability_note: Partial for selected tinea-corporis identity, exposure, lesion-feature, extent, and testing documentation. It does not support UAE pathways, autonomous diagnosis, medicine, dose, treatment, referral urgency, or fixed follow-up.

exact_sections.heading: At a glance, overview, types, and transmission
exact_sections.locator: official page sections “At a glance” through “How it spreads”
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports ringworm, tinea, dermatophyte, circular scaly rash, body-site terminology, spread, person, animal, and contaminated-surface exposure context.

exact_sections.heading: Clinical features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports erythematous or differently pigmented raised scaly rings, central clearing, multiple or extensive lesions, and exudative lesions when bacterial superinfection has developed.

exact_sections.heading: Testing and diagnosis
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-selected diagnostic testing when suspected ringworm may be confused with eczema, psoriasis, or another skin condition; no treatment content is mapped.

title: Clinical Overview of Ringworm
publisher: United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
population: People with suspected ringworm or dermatophyte infection, including skin, scalp, foot, groin, and nail presentations.
intended_setting: Clinical recognition, exposure assessment, and diagnostic testing for suspected fungal skin or nail infection.
